summary: This notebook provides a complete pipeline for the MABe mouse behavior detection competition, combining exploratory data analysis with a hybrid machine learning baseline. It processes 2D tracking coordinates from single-mouse and mouse-pair videos, engineering spatial and temporal features such as squared distances, frame-shifted speeds, and orientation angles. The core model uses LightGBM trained separately for each behavior action, with predictions combined across single and pair configurations. The pipeline includes custom frame-level scoring, threshold-based prediction extraction, and robust post-processing to handle overlapping or missing annotations. Readers should understand how to structure a video tracking baseline, manage memory constraints via downsampling and configuration grouping, and implement competition-specific evaluation metrics.
competition_flow: Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking files, groups videos by body-part configuration, extracts spatial/temporal features for single and pair mice, trains per-action LightGBM classifiers, generates frame-level predictions with thresholding and post-processing, and outputs a CSV submission.
data_reading: pd.read_parquet for tracking coordinates and annotations
data_processing: Drops specific headpiece/tail body parts for memory efficiency
Pivots tracking data and normalizes coordinates by pix_per_cm_approx
Downsamples training data via a custom TrainOnSubsetClassifier
Merges overlapping predictions and fills gaps in post-processing
features_engineering: Squared Euclidean distances between tracked body parts
Cross-mouse squared distances, cross-mouse speed, and relative orientation cosine for pair configurations
ensembling: Combines predictions from separate single-mouse and pair-mouse LightGBM models per action, applies a 0.27 probability threshold, extracts frame transitions, and uses a custom post-processing function to merge overlapping predictions and fill temporal gaps.
insights: Processing data separately by body_parts_tracked configuration is necessary due to varying feature dimensions.
Single-mouse and pair-mouse tracking data provide complementary signals for behavior recognition.
Temporal features like speed and orientation significantly enhance spatial coordinate representations.
Memory constraints require strategic downsampling and dropping less informative body parts.
critical_findings: Some tracking videos contain missing coordinate values that must be handled during pivoting.
The MABe22_ laboratory videos are excluded from processing.
Memory limits necessitate dropping specific headpiece and tail body parts and downsampling training data to 100k samples.
